title: Decision Theoretic Foundations for Conformal Prediction: Optimal Uncertainty Quantification for Risk-Averse Agents

reviews.summary: The paper develops a decision-theoretic foundation for using conformal prediction sets to support risk-averse decision making, where utility is evaluated by value-at-risk (VaR). It proves three core results: (i) given only marginally valid prediction sets, a max–min action policy is minimax optimal over all distributions consistent with the coverage constraint; (ii) optimizing risk-averse policies and utility certificates under a marginal VaR constraint (RA-DPO) is equivalent to optimizing prediction sets and then applying the max–min policy (RA-CPO), establishing prediction sets as a sufficient statistic; and (iii) the optimal prediction sets admit a one-dimensional dual characterization via a scalar β governing how conditional coverage t(x) is allocated across x. Building on these insights, the paper proposes a finite-sample, distribution-free algorithm (RAC) that leverages black-box probabilistic models to construct prediction sets and policies with marginal safety guarantees, demonstrating improved worst-case utility and fewer critical errors on medical diagnosis and recommendation tasks compared to conformal baselines.
strengths: Establishes a clean decision-theoretic link between VaR-based risk-averse decision making and conformal prediction sets: prediction sets are shown to be sufficient statistics for optimal marginal VaR policies.
Proves minimax optimality of a max–min decision rule when acting on sets with only marginal coverage (Proposition 2.2), which is crisp and intuitive for VaR-style guarantees.

Derives a population-level reparameterization in terms of conditional coverage t(x) and an elegant one-dimensional dual characterization t*(x) = g(x, β*), enabling a simple scalar calibration knob to design optimal sets.
Introduces a practical, model-agnostic finite-sample procedure (RAC) with distribution-free marginal safety guarantees that can be combined with any utility function and black-box predictor.

Evaluations on two domains (medical diagnosis with a 4-way action utility matrix; recommendation with binary recommend/abstain actions) using reasonable metrics: realized worst-case (max–min) utility certificate, critical mistake rates, realized utility, and miscoverage.
RAC consistently improves worst-case utility certificates and substantially reduces critical mistakes versus baselines, while meeting coverage targets.

The conceptual pipeline is clearly articulated: define risk-averse target (VaR), prove prediction-set sufficiency and optimality of max–min policy, characterize optimal sets via β, and instantiate a finite-sample algorithm.
The population constructs θ(x, t), a(x, t), and g(x, β) are well motivated and visually supported by helpful figures.
Addresses an important practical gap: systematically connecting uncertainty quantification to downstream, risk-averse utility rather than risk-neutral expectations.

The sufficiency and optimality results justify the use of prediction sets (not just scores) as the right interface for VaR-centric agents and provide a principled way to design them for decision quality, not just coverage.
weaknesses: Finite-sample guarantee: The RAC calibration searches β per candidate label and relies on a split-conformal-style counting argument. However, the paper does not discuss or prove the monotonicity of y ∈ Ĉ(x; β) as a function of β, which is typically crucial for the validity of the “argmin β subject to empirical coverage ≥ 1 − α” construction. Because both â(x, β) and θ̂(x, β) can change non-monotonically with β, inclusion events could be non-monotone, weakening the coverage proof as currently sketched.
Proposition 2.2 assumes α < 0.5 but does not explain why this is needed; the role of this condition in the minimax argument should be clarified or removed if unnecessary.

The one-dimensional dual characterization (Theorem 3.2) requires a continuous marginal P_X and an essentially unique maximizer; the implications of potential ties, non-uniqueness, and measurability issues are not discussed.
No confidence intervals or statistical significance analyses are reported, making it hard to assess robustness of the reported improvements.
Sensitivity to the quality of the black-box probability model f_x is not explored (e.g., under misspecification or covariate shift), though the method depends on it through θ̂ and ĝ.

Comparisons are limited to standard conformal scores and a decision-calibration best-response baseline; more targeted comparisons to decision-aware or risk-aware conformal approaches (e.g., conformal risk control variants, conformal OCE/CVaR control, or closely related minimax set-design methods) are desirable.
Some equations are slightly garbled from PDF extraction (e.g., Eq. (2) typesetting); tie-breaking and quantile conventions could be stated more explicitly.
Algorithmic details for computing ĝ(x, β) and resolving non-uniqueness (and their effect on coverage and utility) could be more thoroughly specified.
There is a growing body of decision-aware uncertainty quantification work closely aligned with this paper’s aims. In particular:

Conformal Risk Control and its variants for controlling general monotone risks (and extensions to OCE/CVaR) provide distribution-free marginal guarantees; the paper cites CRC but a more direct comparison (qualitative or empirical) to end-to-end decision-aware risk control would help.

Concurrent/closely related work on minimax decision rules and set design for expected-loss objectives with a similar one-dimensional dual characterization (e.g., ROCP) highlights a nearby but different risk objective; a clearer comparative discussion would strengthen the positioning.

Action-conditional risk-averse conformal approaches (e.g., AC-RAC) show how to strengthen guarantees conditioned on the taken action; while out of scope here, their relation and trade-offs vs. purely marginal VaR guarantees are worth acknowledging.
reviews.questions:
1. Finite-sample validity: Can you formalize the monotonicity (or provide an alternative exchangeability-based argument) needed for Theorem 4.1 given that Ĉ(x; β) can change non-monotonically in β due to switches in â(x, β) and θ̂(x, β)? If monotonicity fails, what modification to the calibration (e.g., working with a scalar nonconformity score dominating inclusion) restores validity?
2. Proposition 2.2: What role does the assumption α < 0.5 play in the minimax proof? Is it essential or can it be removed/relaxed?
3. Regularity in Theorem 3.2: What precise assumptions ensure existence, measurability, and (almost sure) uniqueness of g(x, β)? How sensitive is RAC’s performance to violations (e.g., frequent ties or non-unique maximizers)?
4. Robustness to f_x: How does RAC behave when the black-box model is miscalibrated or under covariate shift? Can you provide ablations varying calibration set size or perturbing f_x to quantify the utility/coverage trade-offs?
5. Computational aspects: How do you compute ĝ(x, β) and θ̂(x, t) in practice (grid over t, root-finding for β)? What are the computational costs relative to standard split conformal and to the “score-3” decision-focused method?
6. Extensions: Could your dual characterization be adapted to other risk measures like CVaR (beyond VaR)? How would the optimal set structure and the finite-sample calibration need to change?
reviews.assessment:
This paper makes a clear and conceptually elegant contribution by identifying prediction sets as sufficient statistics for marginal VaR-based risk-averse decision making, proving the optimality of a max–min policy on such sets, and deriving a practical, model-agnostic algorithm with distribution-free coverage. The population-level dual characterization via a single scalar β is particularly appealing and provides a principled way to “align” prediction sets with downstream risk-averse utility. Empirical results are encouraging and align with the theory’s focus on worst-case utility and critical-error reduction.

The main technical concern is the finite-sample validity argument: the calibration procedure appears to rely on monotonicity of inclusion in β, which is nontrivial here because both the action and threshold can change with β. Clarifying this point (or adjusting the algorithm to ensure the needed monotonicity or using a different conformal counting argument) is important for correctness. The paper would also benefit from stronger empirical baselines and variance reporting, and from a sharper positioning relative to closely related decision-aware conformal methods with different risk objectives.

Overall, I view the work as a meaningful step toward principled, risk-averse decision making with conformal prediction and recommend acceptance, contingent on addressing the finite-sample validity details and strengthening the empirical study.
